Author: AI
Title: Vibe Coding Your First Databricks App
Text:
Vibe Coding Your First Databricks App
Author: Arnav Chaudhary, Solutions Architect
Introduction:
With the explosion of AI-enabled IDEs such as Cursor, Windsurf, Github Copilot, and Claude Code, software engineering and application development have never been more approachable. “Vibe Coding” is all the hype right now and was anecdotally coined by Andrej Karpathy who used the term to describe the rapid development loop that these AI-enabled IDEs unlock for developers of all skill levels.
The goal of this guide is to make Vibe Coding your first Databricks App feel approachable. I hope to help beginners set up their local IDE, test a development loop from local to Databricks Workspace, and help you deploy your first Databricks App for user feedback.
If you’re a data professional who’s ever thought, “I wish I could build a lightweight web app for my business stakeholders, but I don’t know Dash, FastAPI/Flask, or React,” this guide is for you. We’ll use pure Python, AI assistance, and the power of Databricks Apps to go from zero to a production-ready application in under an hour.
Getting Started: What Are We Building?
At the end of this blog, you should be able to deploy your own Databricks App using Dash. The app will showcase the Enterprise Readiness of Databricks Apps and will showcase the flexibility and power of building applications close to your data platform. We will leverage 3 core components of the Databricks Platform:
- Interactive AI / BI Dashboard: Display a Databricks AI / BI Dashboard (+ Genie) embedded directly into your Application.
- Chatbot Agent: Powered by Genie + Agent Bricks Multi-Agent Supervisor for natural language data queries.
- Databricks Model Serving: Powers the chat bot we will embed in our application.
Think of this overview as your introduction to building Production applications. Databricks Apps allow you to quickly build and iterate on secure, enterprise grade apps as they come with built-in governance through Unity Catalog and secure user authentication through OIDC/OAuth 2.0 + SSO. Furthermore, as the individual Databricks components that your App showcases improve, your App will drive incremental business value.
Why This Architecture Matters
The three services we’re deploying serve distinct but complementary purposes. AI/BI Dashboards provide operational insights and trend analysis for visual thinkers. The chatbot enables conversational data exploration for those who think in questions. Deploying through Model Serving with Agent Bricks unlocks MLflow & Agent Framework so you can track and monitor quality in production.
Databricks Apps help us as developers by abstracting the integration and deployment process. Authentication flows, secure endpoint communication, environment management are all handled automatically by the platform. You write Python code; the Databricks Apps handles the enterprise requirements.
This represents a shift in how data teams deliver value. Instead of users adapting to tools, we’re building tools that adapt to users. Your app becomes a custom interface into your data platform and allows you to meet your users based on their preference through charts, conversations, or customized data driven workflows.
Prerequisites:
Let’s get your environment ready. Don’t worry if some of these tools are new to you, vibe coding is all about learning & incrementally improving your capabilities.
- Databricks Workspace
- AI-Enabled IDE Access (Cursor, Github Copilot, Windsurf)
- Purpose-Built Genie Space
- Agent Bricks — MultiAgent Supervisor created & served
- Python 3.11+ Installed Locally
Why Dash?
We will stick to Dash for the purposes of this guide because it’s the most approachable framework for data personas who haven’t built Apps before but allows enough flexibility to get complex as you iterate. Databricks Apps do support more advanced hybrid architectures (i.e. React.js front-end + FastAPI backend). Furthermore, Dash has a rich visualization ecosystem, integrates easily with Databricks, and enables automatic state management through callbacks.
If you’re familiar with pandas & matplotlib, you already know ~80% of what you need for Dash!
Step-by-Step Implementation:
Step 1: Setting Up Your Databricks CLI & Authentication
First, let’s establish a connection between your local machine and your Databricks workspace. This is essential to support rapid, local dev-loop + Databricks deployment testing.
# Install Databricks CLI if you haven't already
pip install databricks-cli
# Configure your connection
databricks configure
When prompted, enter:
- Databricks Host: Your workspace URL (e.g., https://dbc-123456.cloud.databricks.com)
- Personal Access Token: Generate from User Settings → Developer → Access Tokens
Verify your setup:
# Check your configured profiles
databricks auth profiles
# List workspace contents to confirm connection
databricks workspace list /
Step 2: Initialize Your Databricks App Project
Next we’ll go ahead and create a blank directory on our local machine.
# Create a new directory for your app
mkdir my-first-databricks-app
cd my-first-databricks-app
From your Databricks Workspace, navigate to Compute and click Apps on the top right of the panel. From there, click “Create App”, then select “Dash” as the framework, “ChatBot” as the template, and choose your Multi-Agent endpoint from the dropdown.
This should bring up the following screen:
We can execute the Databricks workspace export-dir command in our local terminal now.
databricks workspace export-dir /Workspace/Users/your.email@company.com/apps/your-app-name ./
This will download the complete app structure that Databricks created for you. AI-IDE’s benefit significantly from having a barebones template to start from. This helps narrow their starting scope which benefits the developer in terms of the type of code the AI generates.
Step 3: Set Up UV Package Manager
We’ll use UV for package management. UV is written in Rust and claims to be 10–100x faster than PIP for dependency loading.
# Install UV
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
# Initialize UV in your project
uv init .
# Create and activate a virtual environment
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate # On Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
# Install dependencies at lightning speed
# First, find the requirements.txt in your exported app
uv pip install -r requirements.txt
UV will make your development experience faster and simpler.
Step 4: Your First Vibe Coding Prompt — Modify Model Serving Endpoint
When you run the app now, you’ll encounter a common development challenge. Let’s see what happens: Open Cursor in your directory from the terminal:
cursor
In the Cursor Window go to the top bar > Terminal > New Terminal. This will open up a terminal in the IDE. Go ahead and execute in the Terminal: uv run python app.py .
You should see an error message pop up that says:
AssertionError: Unable to determine serving endpoint to use for chatbot app.
If developing locally, set the SERVING_ENDPOINT environment variable to the
name of your serving endpoint. If deploying to a Databricks app,
include a serving endpoint resource named 'serving_endpoint'
with CAN_QUERY permissions, as described in
https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/chat-app#deploy-the-databricks-app
This is expected! The Databricks template assumes you’re running in the Databricks environment where endpoints are automatically configured. When developing locally, we need to tell the app how to find your endpoint. Let’s use Cursor to fix this. Copy the following prompt. Make sure you have “Agent” for the Mode and “claude-4-sonnet” for the model. You can also set the Model to “Auto”, but I prefer claude-4-sonnet for beginner coding assistance.
"I have a Databricks chatbot app that I need to run both locally for development and deploy as a Databricks app. I'm getting this error when trying to run it locally: AssertionError: Unable to determine serving endpoint to use for chatbot app. If developing locally, set the SERVING_ENDPOINT environment variable to the name of your serving endpoint. If deploying to a Databricks app, include a serving endpoint resource named 'serving_endpoint' with CAN_QUERY permissions
My serving endpoint is: <REPLACE_WITH_ENDPOINT>
I'm using uv for package management (I have a uv.lock file). Can you:
1. Fix local development setup so I can test with uv run python app.py
2. Fix the Databricks deployment config - so it works when deployed as a Databricks app
3. Update all documentation to use uv commands instead of pip
4. Make it work seamlessly in both environments without manual configs.
I want to be able to run locally for testing, then deploy to Databricks without any code changes."
The app should now start! Visit the local link and try the chatbot. You’ll likely see a “400 Client Error” when sending messages — that’s normal. The Dash template doesn’t know the exact format your AgentBricks endpoint expects. We’ll fix this in Step 6 after adding the dashboard functionality.
Step 5: Embedding AI / BI Dashboards
This prompt will help you embed an AI / BI Dashboard with Tab Navigation.
Cursor will analyze the existing Databricks App Template code and suggest modifications. It will also be smart enough to add a specific configuration for when you’re testing locally versus when you’re testing the App deployed to your Databricks workspace. You will need to update the Dashboard URLs above. You can find this by going to your Dashboard > Share > Embed Dashboard > Iframe > Copy URL under “src”.
Step 6: Adding a ChatBot Powered By Agent Bricks
Next we will create an interactive chat box in our App which will be connected to a Databricks Multi-Agent Supervisor. This Agent Brick will orchestrate a Genie Agent (for structured data retrieval), a Vector Search Agent (for unstructured data retrieval), and can connect to MCP Servers and Unity Catalog Tools / Functions. Multi-Agent Supervisor is a great way to build a Compound AI System with sub-agent orchestration effectively managed by Databricks. Agent Bricks also come out of the box with a MLflow experiment tied to them so you can manage Agent quality.
Here’s the prompt you can use to create a slick interactive chat box which provides an interactive end user experience. This should also resolve the ChatBot error that occurred in Step 4 when you typed something into the box.
Test this prompt by running “uv run python app.py” in the Cursor Terminal.
Step 7: Deploy Your App to Databricks
Go to your Databricks App page (screenshot above) and copy the “databricks sync . . .” command. Execute this in your Cursor terminal. This will sync your local repo up to the Databricks App. Click deploy and you should see that the App runs successfully!
Conclusion:
In under an hour, you’ve transformed from someone who thought web development was out of reach to someone who’s deployed a production-ready Databricks App. You didn’t need to learn React, struggle with authentication, or worry about infrastructure. You just described what you wanted in natural language and refined it through iteration.
Your app now combines three powerful capabilities that solve real business problems:
- Embedded dashboards that bring insights directly to users.
- Conversational AI that democratizes data access through natural language.
- Snappy & Creative ChatBot for an interactive user experience.
But here’s the real magic: As Databricks continues to enhance AI/BI dashboards, improve Agent Bricks, and expand Unity Catalog capabilities, your app automatically inherits these improvements. You’ve built on a platform that evolves with you.
Your next steps:
- Share this app with a business stakeholder and gather feedback
- Add one custom feature based on their input using vibe coding
- Iterate and enhance your app with new capabilities as they come to mind
The barrier between your ideas and deployed applications has never been lower. Stay tuned for Part 2 as we dive deeper into Vibe Coding best practices and build more advanced features.
